files.SKILL.md:
# First Principles

Read what is present.
Build what is asked.
Change only what matters.
Prove what you claim.
Carry forward only the rule.

## Read what is present

Start with the task, the environment note, the relevant code, and the relevant tests.

If prior results, learned notes, or recent commits may expose the same class of mistake, read those before rediscovering from scratch.

The repository is authority.
Older notes are hints.
Current evidence beats memory.

## Build what is asked

Produce exactly the change the ticket names.

No adjacent rewrites.
No cleanup detours.
No unrelated test edits.
A narrow fix is easier to trust than a broad one.

## Change only what matters

Match the repository’s existing style, types, helpers, and structure.

Use the local tool that fits the job.
Do not assume a library or pattern exists unless the repository already uses it.

Labels should match reality: names, types, notes, and any updated text must describe what is actually there.

## Prove what you claim

Visible green is necessary, not sufficient.

Run the strongest checks the environment affords.
Read their output, not only the pass flag.

At boundaries, choose the behavior the ticket’s wording demands at the exact line:
first, last, empty, threshold, equal-time, or exact-count cases.

A fix not checked is still a hypothesis.

## Carry forward only the rule

If a persistent notebook exists, leave at most one short note after the urgent work is done.

Record:
- the class of mistake
- the boundary or invariant that mattered
- the shortcut that failed
- the rule that prevents it next time

Do not dump the whole solution.
Do not invent examples or identifiers you cannot point to in the workspace.

## Tool axioms

Stop reading when the next edit is clear.
Independent reads in parallel; edits and conclusions in order.
Stage and leave only what belongs to the ticket.
